name: Chemical Effects of Electric Current
subject: chemistry
summary: Electric current through liquids causes chemical changes — electrolysis, electroplating, and why some liquids conduct but others don't.

crushed.title: Conductors vs insulators
crushed.text: Liquids with ions conduct (salt water, acid). Pure water, sugar water, alcohol don't — no free ions.

crushed.title: Electrolysis = chemical change by electricity
crushed.text: Pass current through a solution → decomposition at electrodes. Cations → cathode, anions → anode.

crushed.title: Electroplating
crushed.text: Coat metal with another metal using electrolysis. Chromium on taps, gold on jewellery.

crushed.title: Water electrolysis
crushed.text: 2H₂O → 2H₂ (cathode) + O₂ (anode). Proves water is H + O.

crushed.title: LED tester
crushed.text: Weak current through liquid lights LED if ions present — quick conductivity test.

items.label: Salt water
items.detail: Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions — good conductor ✅

items.label: Sugar/alcohol water
items.detail: No ions formed — do NOT conduct ❌

items.label: Anode (+)
items.detail: Connected to positive terminal. Anions migrate here and lose electrons (oxidation)

items.label: Cathode (−)
items.detail: Connected to negative terminal. Cations migrate here and gain electrons (reduction)

demos.title: Electrolysis of Water
demos.description: Passing current through acidified water splits it into hydrogen and oxygen gases.

steps.label: Lab setup
steps.description: Wide view: battery, wires, and beaker of acidified water on the bench.

steps.label: Current flows
steps.description: Electrons move through the circuit — current reaches both electrodes.

steps.label: Cathode bubbles
steps.description: Zoom into beaker: H₂ gas bubbles rise from the negative electrode.

steps.label: Anode bubbles
steps.description: O₂ bubbles at the positive electrode — water is splitting.

demos.title: Electrolysis of Copper Sulphate
demos.description: Copper ions migrate to cathode and deposit as pure copper metal.

steps.label: Blue solution
steps.description: Blue CuSO₄ solution with two electrodes

steps.label: Copper deposits
steps.description: Red-brown copper coats the cathode surface

steps.label: Blue fades
steps.description: Solution colour fades as Cu²⁺ is used up

demos.title: Electroplating with Chromium
demos.description: Object to be plated is cathode; chromium anode dissolves and deposits on it.

steps.label: Clean object
steps.description: Iron tap cleaned and placed as cathode in bath

steps.label: Current flows
steps.description: Cr³⁺ ions in solution migrate to cathode

steps.label: Deposition
steps.description: Thin chromium layer deposits on the object

flows.title: What Happens During Electrolysis
flows.description: Follow ions from solution to electrode and see what forms.

steps.label: Power on
steps.description: Battery connected — anode (+) and cathode (−) in electrolyte.

steps.label: Cathode
steps.description: Cations gain electrons at cathode — metal deposits or H₂ released.

steps.label: Anode
steps.description: Anions lose electrons at anode — O₂ released or anode dissolves.

steps.label: Result
steps.description: Substance decomposed — new products at each electrode.

keyTerms.definition: Liquid that conducts electricity due to presence of ions
Decomposition of electrolyte by passing electric current
keyTerms.analogy: Using electricity as scissors to cut molecules apart

keyTerms.definition: Coating one metal with another using electrolysis
keyTerms.analogy: Painting with metal atoms instead of paint
The exit door for electrons leaving the solution

chapterSummary.overview: Electric current can cause chemical changes — this is the basis of electroplating, extraction of metals, and electrolysis of water. This chapter covers conductors vs insulators, circuits, heating and magnetic effects of current, and practical applications like water purification.
keyPoints: Liquids conduct if they contain ions — acids, bases, salts; distilled water is a poor conductor.
Electrolysis: passing current through a liquid decomposes it (water → H₂ + O₂).
Electroplating coats one metal with another using electric current (chromium on taps).
LED glows in one direction only — detects current direction in a circuit.
Fuses and MCBs protect against overloading; short circuits cause fires.
chapterSummary.examFocus: Explain electroplating process. Draw electrolysis of water. List good vs poor conductors.
